---
title: "Data, Evidence, and Decision Making (with R)"
author: "Ayush Patel"
subtitle: "Session 5: Graphs As Arguments"
institute: "National Law School of India University, Bengaluru"
date: today
date-format: "DD MMM, YYYY"
embed-resources: true
bibliography: citations.bib
execute: 
  echo: false
format: 
  revealjs:
    fig-height: 6
    fig-width: 14
    fig-retina: 1
    embed-resources: true
    margin-left: 50px
    margin-right: 50px
    slide-number: c/t
    width: 1400
    height: 850
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    footer: "Email: ayush.ap58@gmail.com"
---

```{r}
#| label: setup-libs

library(tidyverse)
library(hrbrthemes)
library(ggtext)
```

# Readings

Did you complete these readings?
  

1. Data Visualization a Practical Introduction, Chapter 1 : Look at data @healy2019socviz
2. How Not to Be Wrong: The Power of Mathematical Thinking, Chapter 4: How Much is That in Dead Americans? @ellenberg2014hownotwrong

# Why *look* at data?

  
> Isn't looking at number enough?

## Anscombe as reminder

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column}
```{r}
#|echo: false

anscombe
```
:::

::: {.column}
```{r}
#| echo: false

dplyr::summarise(
  anscombe,
  dplyr::across(
    dplyr::contains("y"),
    list(
      mean = ~ mean(.x),
      sd = ~ sd(.x)
    )
  )
)|>tidyr::pivot_longer(cols = dplyr::everything(),
                       names_sep = "_",names_to = c("variable","measure"))|>
  tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = measure,values_from = value)
```

Corr x1, y1: `r cor(anscombe$x1,anscombe$y1)`\
Corr x2, y2: `r cor(anscombe$x2,anscombe$y2)`\
Corr x3, y3: `r cor(anscombe$x3,anscombe$y3)`\
Corr x4, y4: `r cor(anscombe$x4,anscombe$y4)`
:::
::::

::: {.notes}
Ansocome is a "manufactured and extreme" example, as @healy2019socviz puts it, but there are real examples too.
:::

## Looking at the Quartet

![[Anscombe's quartet-from Data Visualization by Healy](https://socviz.co)](https://socviz.co/generated-figures/fig-ch-01-anscombe.png)

## A Real World Example 

See @healy2019socviz, chapter 1, for details on how this graph challenged a proposed significant association between income inequality and voter turn out.

![[Data Visualization by Healy](https://socviz.co)](https://socviz.co/assets/fig-ch-01-jackman-outlier.png)

# Good Data Viz

  
> Helps to better understand the real word, spot patterns, and ask questions.

## Remember the killer doctor

```{r}
#| label: fig-replicative-shipman-time
#| fig-cap: A replication of death time for Harold Shipman's victims.
#| echo: false

read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dspiegel29/ArtofStatistics/refs/heads/master/00-2-shipman-times/00-2-shipman-times-x.csv") |>
  ggplot(aes(Hour, Shipman)) + 
  geom_line() +
  geom_line(aes(Hour, Comparison), linetype = 2) + 
  labs(
    title = "Comparing time of death",
    subtitle = "Solid line is for deaths of Shipman's victims. Dotted line is death count of comparison group",
    x = "Hour of the day",
    y = "Number of deaths",
    caption = "Data Source: Github dspiegel29/ArtofStatistics and The Art of Statistics"
  )+
  theme_minimal()

```

# What makes a good data viz?

## It is not easy to define

  
[No mechanical set of rules that can be universally applied]{.fragment fragment-index='1'}  
  
[Effectiveness is dependent on many factors: Audience, medium, why thy are looking at it, etc.]{.fragment fragment-index='2'}  
  
[*Some part* of learning what is good data viz is about developing a good taste.]{.fragment fragment-index='3'}  
  
[it also involves learning about perception and good practices]{.fragment fragment-index='4'}  
  
[While some rules can be broken, one must know exactly when, why, and how?]{.fragment fragment-index='5'}  
  
[**Learning what is good data viz also involves learning to identify what is bad data viz**]{.fragment fragment-index='6'}


# What makes a bad graph?

## Three kinds of errors

There can be one or a combination of the following kinds of errors in a chart.

<br><br>
[Aesthetic]{.fragment fragment-index='1'}
<br><br>
[Substantive]{.fragment fragment-index='2'}
<br><br>
[Perceptual]{.fragment fragment-index='3'}

## Aesthetic - Bad Taste

<br><br>

[Redundant elements]{.fragment fragment-index='1'}
<br><br>
[Too much junk]{.fragment fragment-index='2'}
<br><br>
[very low data to ink ratio]{.fragment fragment-index='3'}
<br><br>
[Over burdens the readers cognitive load]{.fragment fragment-index='4'}

## Aesthetic - Bad Taste

![[From FlowingData](https://flowingdata.com/)](https://flowingdata.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/04/Changing-face.jpg)

## Aesthetic - Bad Taste

![[From FlowingData](https://flowingdata.com/)](https://flowingdata.com/wp-content/uploads/2013/07/Speaking-the-world-620x617.jpg)

## Substantive - Error in data or its presentation


![[From FlowingData](https://flowingdata.com/)](https://flowingdata.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/05/inflation-cherrypicking-750x761.png)

## Substantive - NYT example from @healy2019socviz

![What can you say about and from this chart?](https://socviz.co/assets/fig-ch-01-democracy-nyt-version.png)

## Substantive - NYT example from @healy2019socviz

What if I told you?

<br><br>

[These are are not the responses from a longitudinal survey]{.fragment fragment-index='1'}
<br><br>
[Actually, it is the same question asked to people born in different decades, i.e., different age groups.]{.fragment fragment-index='2'}
<br><br>
[Cherry on top it was not a binary question]{.fragment fragment-index='3'}
<br><br>
[Identifying substantive problems require understanding of underlying data of a chart, being observant of any transformations and consequent effects, etc..]{.fragment fragment-index='4'}

## Substantive - NYT example from @healy2019socviz

![Voeten's correction](https://socviz.co/assets/fig-ch-01-democracy-voeten-version-2.png)

## Perception

![[From FlowingData](https://flowingdata.com/)](https://flowingdata.com/wp-content/uploads/2013/08/Gratuitous-pies.png)

## Perception 

![[From FlowingData](https://flowingdata.com/)](https://flowingdata.com/wp-content/uploads/2012/04/3d-column-chart-of-awesome.png)

## Perception 

![[From FlowingData](https://flowingdata.com/)](https://flowingdata.com/wp-content/uploads/2012/04/exploding-pie-charts-ftw.png)

## Perception

```{r}
#| label: bristol-truncated
#| fig-dpi: 500

read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dspiegel29/ArtofStatistics/refs/heads/master/01-1-2-3-child-heart-survival-times/01-1-child-heart-survival-x.csv")  |>
  ggplot(aes(y = Hospital, x = ThirtyDaySurvival)) + 
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") + 
  labs(
    title = "Share of children surviving 30 days of heart surgery",
    y = NULL,
    x = "Proportion of children",
    caption = "Data Source: Github Repo dspiegel29/ArtofStatistics"
      ) + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(95,100)) +
  theme_ipsum_es(grid = "x")   

```

## Perception {.nostretch}

```{r}
#| label: bristol-not-truncated
#| fig-dpi: 500

read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dspiegel29/ArtofStatistics/refs/heads/master/01-1-2-3-child-heart-survival-times/01-1-child-heart-survival-x.csv")  |>
  ggplot(aes(Hospital, ThirtyDaySurvival)) + 
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") + 
  labs(
    title = "Share of children surviving 30 days of heart surgery",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Proportion of children",
    caption = "Data Source: Github Repo dspiegel29/ArtofStatistics"
      ) + 
  theme_ipsum_es(grid = "X") + 
  coord_flip()
```

# FLOWINGDATA

See [here](https://flowingdata.com/projects/dishonest-charts/). A resource to visually and interactively understand how *dishonest* charts mislead.

# Discussion

<br><br>

> How Not to Be Wrong: The Power of Mathematical Thinking, Chapter 4: How Much is That in Dead Americans? @ellenberg2014hownotwrong

# Resources

1. [ggplot wizardry by Dr Cedric Scherer](https://www.cedricscherer.com/slides/useR-2021_ggplot-wizardry-extended.pdf)
2. [On neutrality of reader and conformation bias]( https://nightingaledvs.com/we-taught-students-to-read-graphs-but-we-forgot-to-teach-them-to-question-themselves/)

# End of session

# References
